speaker: Jenny Wen
text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.
summary: AI makes starting companies trivially easy, but the resulting explosion of competition makes scaling harder than ever.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.

speaker: Sherwin Wu

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The market is immutable. No amount of tweeting, advertising is going to change whether the market wants your product.
summary: Product-market fit is like drug receptor binding — the market wants your product or it does not.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Point solutions don't have enough data in the age of AI to be useful. If you own the mine with the data, you can make money.
summary: In the AI era, point SaaS solutions lack sufficient data context — only companies owning first-party data capture value.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The purest form of ambition and most intense source of energy in the business is the founder CEO. Every next concentric circle of management has the potential to be an order of magnitude drop off in intensity.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: The best growth teams I've seen all have one thing in common: they measure retention before acquisition. If your bucket is leaky, pouring more water in doesn't help.

speaker: Elena Verna

summary: Good gamification makes progress visible through mechanics like leaderboards and streaks, not superficial badges.

speaker: Elena Verna

summary: Notifications should be product decisions, not marketing — most companies underinvest in notification quality.

speaker: Edwin Chen
text: We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game. We could fire 90% of the people and we would move faster.

text: Do you want a model that says 'There are definitely 20 more ways to improve this email' or one that's optimizing for your time and just says 'Your email's great, just send it'?

speaker: Edwin Chen

text: There's actually three levels to listening. Level one is internal. Level two is focused. Level three is global listening — you hear what they're communicating, not just saying.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: I use a GROW model. G is goal, R is reality, O is options, W is way forward.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: We operate in tech. We're supposed to give all of ourselves purely logically. That's not at all true. The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.

speaker: Rachel Lockett

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? That's the question we always asked at Stripe.

summary: Founders must create both the product and the market, positioning through familiar concepts.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: The AI models that you're using today is the worst AI model you will ever use for the rest of your life. Every two months, computers can do something they've never been able to do before and you need to completely think differently about what you're doing.
summary: Today's AI model is the worst you will ever use; capabilities leap forward every two months requiring constant rethinking of what is possible.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: We have this philosophy of model maximalism. Our general mindset is in two months there's going to be a better model and it's going to blow away whatever the current set of limitations are. If you're building right on the edge of the capabilities, keep going because you're doing something right.
summary: OpenAI's model maximalism philosophy means building at the frontier of model capabilities, trusting that the next model will unlock what barely works today.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: You're trying to figure out how some product should work with AI, you can often reason about it the way you would reason about another human and it works. If I asked you something that I needed to think for 20 seconds to answer, I wouldn't just go mute. I also wouldn't start babbling every single thought.
summary: The most counterintuitive AI product design insight is that reasoning about how a human would behave in the same situation often gives the right UX answer.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: My personal belief is that you want to be pretty PM light as an organization. Too many PMs causes problems. We'll fill the world with decks and ideas versus execution. It's a good thing when a PM is working with slightly too many engineers.
summary: OpenAI keeps PMs intentionally scarce so they cannot micromanage; slightly overstretched PMs empower product-focused engineers to make decisions.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: I actually think chat is an amazing interface because it's so versatile. It is the way we talk. If I had some more rigid interface, I would be able to speak to you about far fewer things and it would get in the way of maximum communication bandwidth.
summary: Chat is not a placeholder interface for AI but a fundamentally powerful one because it matches how humans naturally communicate with zero restrictions.

speaker: Kevin Weil
text: We use ensembles of models much more internally than people might think. If we have 10 different problems, we might solve them using 20 different model calls, some using specialized fine-tuned models, different sizes for different latency or cost requirements.
summary: OpenAI internally uses ensembles of specialized, fine-tuned models of different sizes rather than one generic model for everything.

speaker: Wes Kao
text: You can continue to think that way and your career might be fine, but if you embrace that if you manage your boss, they're going to appreciate you much more, you're going to get more opportunities, you're going to have more trust with them.
summary: Managing up is not about sucking up; it is a strategic skill where proactively keeping your boss informed builds trust and creates more opportunities.

speaker: Wes Kao
text: As you move up the triangle of the content hierarchy of BS, there's less and less room for BS. In a cohort-based course where your students are right there with you, if you're saying something that doesn't really make sense, there could be a whole conversation happening in Zoom chat.
summary: The content hierarchy of BS framework shows that interactive formats like courses expose weak thinking, while tweets and short posts can hide behind clever phrasing.

speaker: Wes Kao
text: The idea behind the state change method is that you should punctuate your monologues with state changes. State changes are anything that shakes your audience awake and adds variety. Every three to five minutes, go ahead and put in a state change.
summary: The state change method combats audience fatigue by inserting engagement shifts like polls, chat prompts, or screen shares every three to five minutes.

speaker: Wes Kao
text: When you're talking to someone and explaining something, there's usually a moment where their eyes light up because they are genuinely interested. Make note of those moments because their face can't lie. Cut out all the parts that make people go dead in the eyes.
summary: The eyes-light-up method teaches you to watch for genuine reactions during conversations and double down on what truly resonates.

speaker: Wes Kao
text: Alex would always talk about trade-offs. He'd say, 'Yes, I can design this PDF for you. That means the thing I was going to work on today will have to wait.' It went from a conversation about yes or no into how do we make sure the right things get done.
summary: The best way to say no at work is to frame it as trade-offs, letting the requester help you prioritize rather than making it a binary yes or no.

speaker: Ryan Singer
text: If you're doing a home renovation, you can have the most beautiful rendering of the new bedroom. But if you haven't checked if there's electricity in that wall, it's going to drastically change the cost and the time and everything.
summary: Shape Up's core insight is that beautiful plans are worthless without checking technical feasibility first, just like a renovation rendering without checking for wiring.

speaker: Ryan Singer
text: We are not going to start something unless we can see the end from the beginning. We're going to go the other way around and say, what is the maximum amount of time we're willing to go before we actually finish something? Six weeks is the maximum we can see into the future.
summary: Shape Up inverts traditional planning: instead of estimating how long a feature takes, you fix the time budget at six weeks maximum and vary the scope.

speaker: Ryan Singer
text: Instead of 'here's your ticket' or 'here's your user story,' it's 'here's the thing you understand, that makes sense, and now you're going to have freedom to figure out how to actually make this a reality.' We see way more engagement, especially from the technical team.
summary: Giving teams the whole shaped idea rather than pre-shredded tickets dramatically increases engineer engagement and ownership.

speaker: Ryan Singer
text: Those sessions with Jason, they were short, very intense sessions where you're trying to crack the nut together. It wasn't sitting alone writing a document, it wasn't making a bunch of requirements. It wasn't making a beautiful Figma file. It was this super intense collaborative 'what about this? what about that?'
summary: Shaping sessions are intense collaborative whiteboard sessions to crack an idea's core, not solo document writing or polished Figma files.

speaker: Ryan Singer
text: Six weeks is only a maximum. If we think of six weeks as a maximum, that's going to force us to ask some really good questions about what piece of this do we really think we can land. If you try to say in six months we're going to ship this thing, you can't get your arms around all the problems.
summary: The six-week maximum is a ceiling that forces teams to surface unknowns and complexity before building, not an arbitrary sprint length.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: Our engineering goal is every engineer should ship a marketable product every week.
summary: Captions sets the extreme pace of every engineer shipping a marketable product feature every single week.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: Every piece of debt that you take on you have to pay interest on. 1% or 2% of your time every day. If you take on enough debt, you'll be paying 80 or 90% interest and you won't have time to do anything new. You have a technical debt runway.
summary: Technical debt works like financial debt with daily interest payments; too much debt means all your time goes to maintenance with nothing left for building.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: We have a second roadmap which we think of as a secret roadmap. Nobody has ever asked for anything on it. Given our unique vantage point, we've come up with special ideas that will completely revolutionize how something is used where we can truly change the behavior of the user.
summary: Captions maintains a secret roadmap of ideas no user has asked for, based on the team's unique insight, which produces their biggest wins.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: The design team was 10, 12 people at 5,000-6,000 employees. For a long time there were no PMs at all. These weren't your average designers. These were designers who were actually PMs as well. That's what the secret sauce was.
summary: Snap's innovation engine was a tiny design team of 10-12 designer-PMs who controlled product at 6,000 employees, giving the CEO granular control over every user-facing change.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: As the company gets bigger, you can actually create alignment by causing internal virality. We would create prototype products, share the build, and it would explode inside the company. Day after day we would hear from engineers, then managers, then VPs, then eventually from Evan.
summary: At Snap, the design engineering team created internal virality with prototypes that spread organically through the company, achieving instant alignment without formal processes.

speaker: Gaurav Misra
text: At Captions, we're going even one step further. Why shouldn't the PM understand marketing? I actually think PMs should own all the way to marketing. Search marketing is just placing a button to your product in Google. That's almost a product surface.
summary: PMs should own the full funnel including marketing because ad placements and acquisition are product surfaces that require the same user understanding.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: You have to deliberately not act on the feedback of many of your early users, and this is at the same time as listening to people intensely and building what people want. That's what we're here to do, is to make something that people want, but it can't be all people.
summary: Achieving product-market fit requires deliberately ignoring feedback from users who are not your target audience, even while listening intensely.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: This is a technique that I call the switch lock. It's born out of the observation that your calendar says what you thought you were going to do, but it's really only your trail of work that describes what you actually did. How can we capture that? So I came up with the following idea. What if I just did whatever the heck I wanted?
summary: The 'switch lock' technique means tracking what you actually do rather than what your calendar says, letting you focus on what matters most.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: You can classify anything that you build in a company into one of two categories, solution deepening and market widening. Solution deepening means making your product better for its existing users, but not making it available to more users. Whereas market widening means making your product available to more users, but not making the product itself any better.
summary: Every company effort falls into either solution deepening (better product for existing users) or market widening (reaching new users), and balancing the two is critical to sustained growth.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: The biggest secret of Superhuman, which is something that nobody else does, is that we would manually onboard every single new user for the first five years. People would sign up on the wait list, and when we were ready, our growth team would reach out, schedule a call, get on Zoom, and walk the person through every feature, every shortcut, and every workflow for Superhuman.
summary: Superhuman manually onboarded every user one-on-one for five years, which drove deep engagement and word-of-mouth growth.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: I believe that every product should be positioned around a single primary attribute. In our case, that attribute is speed. Now, lots of products try to be good at everything. That tends to be a mistake, because if you try to stand for everything, you stand for nothing.
summary: Successful product positioning means choosing a single primary attribute and committing fully to it rather than trying to be good at everything.

speaker: Rahul Vohra
text: If you haven't tried Superhuman, then gosh, what are you doing? Getting through your email twice as fast, responding one to two days sooner, saving four hours or more every single week, they're all real.
summary: Superhuman's core value proposition is measurable: users get through email twice as fast and save four-plus hours per week.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: The company was on the verge of going under when we launched Bolt, and what ended up happening is, in the first two months it went from zero to 20 million of ARR. And we've already crossed 30 million of ARR, with the current rate we're on, our forecast for the year is we want to get to 100 million of ARR.
summary: Bolt went from near-death to $20M ARR in two months, becoming one of the fastest-growing products in startup history.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: Most importantly, it's been the people. It's rare to find startups where you have the core group of five, six, seven people that have been there for five years plus.
summary: Bolt's explosive growth was enabled by a rare core team of five to seven people who had worked together for over five years.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: Sonnet was really the first model that flipped the equation. We actually tried building Bolt almost exactly a year ago, with the frontier models at the time. Spent a week or two building it. It just didn't work. The output, the code output was not reliable enough. And then we got a sneak peek of the Sonnet stuff in May and we were like, 'Oh. Okay, we should take that project back off the shelf.'
summary: Anthropic's Sonnet model was the breakthrough that made Bolt possible -- earlier frontier models produced code that was not reliable enough.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: Software is deterministic. When you write code and you hit run, it either runs or it doesn't. And that's the key insight Anthropic really had. They just went deep. This is what they're doing, is just reinforcement learning on basically permutating every type of app you could ever build. I'm extremely bullish. It makes technical sense why, of anything, LLMs are going to get insanely better at writing code than probably most other types of applications.
summary: Code is uniquely suited for AI because it is deterministic and verifiable, enabling massive reinforcement learning at scale.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: I would say, talk to this thing like you do a Linear ticket, or a JIRA ticket. That would be my advice. Talk to this like you're talking to one of the developers on your team. Be specific on things that matter. And on things where you can let it be creative, you can just say, 'Hey, make it prettier.' And it does a really good job when you give it just, vibes.
summary: The best way for PMs to use AI coding tools is to treat them like a developer teammate: be specific on requirements and give creative freedom on aesthetics.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: On any Figma URL, when you're looking at a design that you've made, if you just put bolt.new in front of that URL and hit enter, it's going to suck that design into Bolt, and turn it into a full stack app or mobile app, just out of the box.
summary: Bolt's Figma integration lets users convert any Figma design URL into a working full-stack application with one click.

speaker: Eric Simons
text: It's always good to just have the perspective of, you should start companies to keep the mindset that you're doing it to have fun. So, stoked to see where this goes, one way or the other. It's going to be interesting.
summary: The right mindset for founders is to build companies to have fun, not just to chase outcomes.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: Lovable is your personal AI software engineer. You describe an idea and then you get a fully working product. The reason is to enable those who have had such a hard time finding people who are good at creating software that's been their absolute bottleneck and let them take their ideas and their dreams into reality.
summary: Lovable removes the bottleneck of finding engineering talent by letting anyone turn an idea description into a working product.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: The best word for a great product is that it's lovable. A lot of jargon that I like to use to emphasize what we should be striving for is building a minimum lovable product and then building a lovable product and then building an absolutely lovable product. So I took that jargon with me in the company name.
summary: The concept of 'minimum lovable product' rather than minimum viable product became the company's identity and north star.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: I think the number one most important skill when AI can do more and more is taste, like having really good taste for what to build and what is a great product. And so in some ways, the people that are like the best product people, like you, I actually think they're going to become more and more important over time.
summary: As AI handles more execution, taste -- knowing what to build and what makes a great product -- becomes the most important human skill.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: Just start simple and then what you get is that the AI says, okay, I can go through what does a beautiful Airbnb clone look like and it goes through a bit of design decisions and then I'll zoom out to see more of it. We have this just UI that is... I mean it has all the nice things you would expect from Airbnb clone where you see different categories and you can see two listings from Airbnb with login buttons and everything.
summary: A two-word prompt ('Airbnb clone') produces a full UI with categories, listings, and login buttons in 30 seconds.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: The big learning is that you have to start with how is this product working end-to-end and then add AI or think where should we add AI. You really want to see what does the big picture of the user experience look like, and then add something with AI to solve specific problems.
summary: Start with the end-to-end user experience first, then add AI to solve specific problems -- not the other way around.

speaker: Anton Osika
text: I think if you spend a full week on trying to reach an outcome, the best way to learn is I want to do this thing and then I want to use AI to do that thing. And you've spent a full week, you are in the top 1% in the global population. And if you surround yourself with friends who have this obsession or they also care a lot about this, then you'd be quickly in the top 0.1%.
summary: Spending just one focused week using AI tools to solve a real problem puts you in the top 1% of AI fluency globally.
